calendar.matters:
City And County Of San Franciscos Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs Submitted By Petitioner Synergy Project Management Inc
Off Calendar Apr-04-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Respondent City and County of San Francisco's motion to tax costs is granted as to $114.90 of the claimed costs for service of process and denied as to all other costs sought to be taxed. Petitioner Synergy Project Management, Inc. is awarded costs of $3062.74. Synergy concedes that it is not entitled to $114.90 of its claimed costs for service of process. All of the other service of process costs challenged by the City should have been sought either under filing fees or other on the costs memorandum, but that error does not deprive Synergy of recovering them since the costs were reasonably incurred. The administrative record costs were also reasonably incurred since the entirety of the proceedings covered by that record were implicated by the parties' arguments. ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/HK)

Petitioner Ghilotti Bros., Inc.'S Motion For Judgment On Writ
Off Calendar Nov-10-2016 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Kris Kox of Moscone Emblidge & Otis LLP telephone number 415-362-3599 for Petitioner Synergy Project Management, Inc.
James Diwik of Sedgwick LLP telephone number 415-781-7900 for Petitioner Ghilotti Bros., Inc.
Anne Johnson of Office of the City Attorney telephone number 415-554-3956 for Respondent City and County of San Francisco.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling.
Petitioner Ghilotti Bros., Inc.'s petition for writ of mandate is granted and the January 8, 2016 Statement of Hearing Officer Findings is vacated. The hearing officer erred in determining that he had jurisdiction to determine whether the Department of Public Works presented sufficient evidence to support its substitution demand. All three bases that the hearing officer offered for his exercise of jurisdiction are erroneous as a matter of law. The parties agree that section 4107of the Public Contract Code by itself is not a basis for jurisdiction where, as here, the proposed substitution of a subcontractor is initiated by the awarding authority, not prime contractor. The parties also agree that, per section 4.04 of the contract between the Department of Public Works and Ghilotti, a hearing to determine the propriety of substituting the subcontractor can only occur if Ghilotti requested the replacement of Synergy. None of the evidence cited by the City to show that Ghilotti made such a request shows that Ghilotti did in fact make such a request. A review of the relevant portions of the Administrative Record fails to disclose any instance where prior to the hearing held by the hearing officer Ghilotti requested that the Department of Public Works replace Synergy as a subcontractor. While Ghilotti investigated potential replacement subcontractors to avoid breaching its contract after the City directed Ghilotti to remove Synergy (AR Tab 83; Hearing Transcript 89:7-16), that is not the same as Ghilotti making a request to the City that Synergy be removed. Nor was there any jurisdiction to hold the hearing simply because such a hearing is not precluded by section 4107. For the hearing officer to have jurisdiction to address the substantive issue he addressed there must be some statute, ordinance, contract or other legal authority providing the hearing officer with such jurisdiction. Because there is none, the hearing officer lacked jurisdiction and thus, per CCP 1094.5, he prejudicially abused his discretion by proceeding in a manner not authorized by law by addressing the issue of whether the Department of Public Works presented sufficient evidence to support its substitution demand. Order signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Kris Kox of Moscone Emblidge & Otis LLP telephone number 415-362-3599 for Petitioner Synergy Project Management, Inc.
James Diwik of Sedgwick LLP telephone number 415-781-7900 for Petitioner Ghilotti Bros., Inc.
Anne Johnson of Office of the City Attorney telephone number 415-554-3956 for Respondent City and County of San Francisco.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling.
Petitioner Synergy Project Management, Inc.'s petition for writ of mandate is granted and the January 8, 2016 Statement of Hearing Officer Findings is vacated. The hearing officer erred in determining that he had jurisdiction to determine whether the Department of Public Works presented sufficient evidence to support its substitution demand. All three bases that the hearing officer offered for his exercise of jurisdiction are erroneous as a matter of law. The parties agree that section 4107of the Public Contract Code by itself is not a basis for jurisdiction where, as here, the proposed substitution of a subcontractor is initiated by the awarding authority, not prime contractor. The parties also agree that, per section 4.04 of the contract between the Department of Public Works and Ghilotti, a hearing to determine the propriety of substituting the subcontractor can only occur if Ghilotti requested the replacement of Synergy. None of the evidence cited by the City to show that Ghilotti made such a request shows that Ghilotti did in fact make such a request. A review of the relevant portions of the Administrative Record fails to disclose any instance where prior to the hearing held by the hearing officer Ghilotti requested that the Department of Public Works replace Synergy as a subcontractor. While Ghilotti investigated potential replacement subcontractors to avoid breaching its contract after the City directed Ghilotti to remove Synergy (AR Tab 83; Hearing Transcript 89:7-16), that is not the same as Ghilotti making a request to the City that Synergy be removed. Nor was there any jurisdiction to hold the hearing simply because such a hearing is not precluded by section 4107. For the hearing officer to have jurisdiction to address the substantive issue he addressed there must be some statute, ordinance, contract or other legal authority providing the hearing officer with such jurisdiction. Because there is none, the hearing officer lacked jurisdiction and thus, per CCP 1094.5, he prejudicially abused his discretion by proceeding in a manner not authorized by law by addressing the issue of whether the Department of Public Works presented sufficient evidence to support its substitution demand. Order signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Issuance Of Writ Of Mandate

HE DOES NOT VIEW THIS AND WILL NOT IMPACT HIS ABILITY TO HEAR THIS MATTER. NO OBJECTIONS WERE MADE BY THE PARTIES. THE MATTER WAS ARGUED.
